typography.heading: Lora
typography.tracking: tight for headings (-0.02em on h1/h2, -0.01em on h3), normal for body (0em), 0.06em letter-spacing on any all-caps labels or eyebrow text

motion.pageEntrance: brief and functional — no animation for its own sake

voice.headline_style: Outcome-led and patient-centered — headlines should name the relief or result the patient gains, not the procedure or technology that delivers it. Conversational enough to feel spoken by a real doctor, not written by a marketing department. The sister-founded, community-native story should surface in how headlines are framed: this is a practice built for people who were previously turned away or underserved, so headlines should implicitly validate that experience before offering a resolution.
voice.cta_language: Active, specific, and low-pressure — CTAs should describe the next concrete step rather than a vague commitment. Language should invite rather than push, reflecting the practice's explicit low-pressure first-visit philosophy and its Tell-Show-Do desensitization approach. Avoid urgency language or sales-pressure phrasing entirely.
voice.tone_notes: Copy should read the way Dr. Tiffany or Dr. Trisha might actually speak to a patient in the room — warm, confident, and technically credible without being jargon-heavy. The practice's clinical depth (Kois Center, Stanford GPR, board-certified surgeon on staff, GentleWave, IV sedation) should come through as quiet authority, not credentials-dumping.

rationale: The existing brand — scattered saturated primaries, no type system, a 100px logo without a surrounding system — was too weak and generic to preserve any element beyond the practice name. The peach-deep palette was selected because its terracotta-to-earth-tone range directly mirrors the emotional register this practice occupies: warm enough to signal safety to anxious patients and families, grounded enough to carry clinical credibility for complex-case positioning. Lora was chosen as the heading font because its humanist serif construction conveys the kind of personal, unhurried care the sister-founders have built their practice around — it would be wrong on a DSO or a high-volume whitening spa, but it is correct here. Source Sans 3 pairs with Lora because it is a large-family humanist sans with exceptional legibility at body sizes, carries warm optical weight that complements Lora without competing, and is not a default pairing seen across commodity dental sites. The 60/30/10 color application puts the warm cream light (#fdf7f3) as the dominant surface, the dark earth (#2e1a0f) and muted (#8a6a58) as structural text and border tones, and the terracotta primary (#c4704a) strictly as CTA, heading emphasis, and key interaction — rare enough to retain visual pull.
paletteSource: peach-deep (Peach and deep earth) — selected because the terracotta-to-warm-earth range is the only palette in the candidate set that simultaneously reads as welcoming to anxious patients and families AND carries enough visual weight to support the practice's advanced-care, complex-case clinical positioning. The blush-rose option felt too spa-adjacent; coral-dark was too energetic for a practice centered on patient calm; honey-oak skewed too rustic. Peach-deep occupies the precise middle ground between comfort and credibility.

failures.note: CTAs / heading text on light background
